family_history: Father: glioblastoma multiforme diagnosed at age 52, deceased at age 54
Maternal aunt: breast cancer at age 48
No family history of autoimmune or demyelinating disease

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social use, 1-2 drinks per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner in urban apartment

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Fluent speech without dysarthria or aphasia. Follows complex three-step commands. Attention intact to serial sevens. Short-term recall 3/3 at registration, 2/3 at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally. Bilateral papilledema on fundoscopic exam with blurred disc margins and venous engorgement. Visual fields full to confrontation. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation. CN V: Intact sensation in all three divisions bilaterally. CN VII: Symmetric facial movements. CN VIII: Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Left upper extremity: 5/5 throughout. Right upper extremity: 4/5 deltoid, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip strength. Left lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Right lower extremity: 4/5 hip flexion, knee extension, ankle dorsiflexion. No pronator drift on the left; subtle right pronator drift present. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Decreased proprioception in the right great toe. Vibration sense intact bilaterally at ankles.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Left biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 2+. Right biceps 3+, triceps 3+, brachioradialis 3+, patellar 3+, Achilles 3+. Right Babinski sign present (extensor plantar response). Left plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose: intact on left, mild past-pointing on right. Heel-to-shin: intact bilaterally. No dysdiadochokinesia. No tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly spastic right-sided gait with circumduction of the right lower extremity. Able to tandem walk with difficulty. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningeal signs. No Lhermitte sign. No Uhthoff phenomenon reported.

patient.chief_complaint: Acute right-sided weakness, headache, and nausea for 1 day
patient.history_present_illness: A 28-year-old previously healthy right-handed Hispanic woman presents to the emergency department with a 1-day history of progressive right-sided weakness, severe headache, and nausea with one episode of vomiting. She reports that the symptoms began yesterday morning upon awakening, initially noticing difficulty gripping her coffee mug with her right hand. Over the course of the day, she developed a progressively worsening holocephalic headache rated 8/10 in severity, accompanied by nausea and photophobia. By the evening, she noted difficulty walking due to weakness in her right leg, prompting her partner to bring her to the emergency department. She denies any preceding febrile illness, recent travel, insect bites, or trauma. She has no prior history of neurological symptoms, seizures, or similar episodes. She was seen by her primary care physician 6 months ago for a routine physical and was in good health. Of note, her father was diagnosed with glioblastoma at age 52 and passed away two years ago, which is causing her significant anxiety about the possibility of a brain tumor. She denies any recent weight loss, night sweats, or changes in appetite. She reports no visual changes, speech difficulties, or sensory symptoms. She has been taking combined oral contraceptives for the past 3 years without complications. On initial assessment in the emergency department, she was hemodynamically stable with mild tachycardia. Neurological examination was notable for right hemiparesis, a right Babinski sign, and bilateral papilledema.

findings.location: Left parietal lobe, involving subcortical and deep white matter, extending to the periventricular region

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense relative to surrounding white matter
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense with incomplete suppression of central portion
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion at the leading edge of the lesion with elevated signal on DWI and corresponding low ADC values
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Incomplete 'C'-shaped or open-ring enhancement pattern, with the open portion facing the cortical gray matter

findings.mass_effect: Moderate mass effect with effacement of adjacent sulci, 6 mm leftward-to-rightward midline shift at the level of the septum pellucidum
findings.borders: Relatively well-defined margins with perilesional edema extending into the corona radiata

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter, adjacent to the body of the lateral ventricle

additional_observations: MR spectroscopy performed: elevated choline peak (Cho/Cr ratio 2.1), presence of a lactate doublet at 1.33 ppm, decreased N-acetylaspartate (NAA/Cr ratio 1.2, below normal), and mildly elevated myo-inositol
No additional enhancing lesions identified in the posterior fossa or brainstem
Ventricles are mildly asymmetric with mild compression of the left lateral ventricle
No evidence of dural-based mass or extra-axial collection
Circle of Willis appears patent on contrast-enhanced sequences
mri.impression: Large heterogeneously enhancing left parietal white matter mass lesion (4.1 x 3.0 x 4.0 cm) with incomplete ring enhancement pattern, restricted diffusion at the leading edge, mass effect, and 6 mm midline shift. MR spectroscopy shows elevated choline, lactate, and decreased NAA. A small non-enhancing T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the right periventricular white matter is also noted. Differential diagnosis includes high-grade glioma (most likely given size, spectroscopy findings, and family history), CNS lymphoma, tumefactive demyelination, and brain abscess. Tissue diagnosis is strongly recommended.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Most likely given lesion size, ring enhancement, elevated choline, decreased NAA, and family history of GBM
Possible but less likely given the open-ring pattern rather than homogeneous enhancement
Cannot be excluded; incomplete ring enhancement and leading-edge restricted diffusion are suggestive features
Less likely in absence of fever, leukocytosis, or infectious source

recommended_actions: Stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection for tissue diagnosis
Consider advanced MRI techniques (perfusion MRI) if available
Neurosurgical consultation urgently given mass effect and midline shift
If clinical deterioration occurs, emergent surgical decompression may be needed

findings: No evidence of chamber enlargement or hypertrophy
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 88 bpm with no acute abnormalities.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No cardiac etiology for the presenting symptoms. Normal ECG does not exclude cardioembolic stroke but makes it less likely.

Tumor Markers.clinical_significance: Normal LDH does not exclude CNS lymphoma

labs.interpretation: All laboratory values are within normal limits including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, coagulation studies, tumor markers, ANA, vitamin B12, and TSH. The normal inflammatory markers and WBC count argue against an infectious etiology. Normal tumor markers are non-specific and do not exclude CNS neoplasm.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Absent in both CSF and serum
special_tests.Cytology: No malignant cells identified. Rare reactive lymphocytes.
special_tests.Flow cytometry: No monoclonal B-cell population identified. Normal CD4:CD8 ratio.

csf.interpretation: CSF shows mild pleocytosis with lymphocytic predominance, mildly elevated protein, and elevated opening pressure consistent with mass effect. Notably, oligoclonal bands are absent and IgG index is normal, which argues against typical MS but does not exclude it (OCBs are absent in approximately 5-10% of confirmed MS cases). Myelin basic protein is mildly elevated, suggesting active demyelination. Cytology and flow cytometry do not support lymphoma or other neoplastic process. The overall CSF profile is non-specific and does not definitively distinguish between tumefactive demyelination and low-grade neoplasm.

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level, dorsal columns

additional_observations: No additional lesions in the thoracic or lumbar spinal cord
Vertebral body heights and alignment are normal
No epidural or intradural extramedullary masses
output.impression: Single non-enhancing T2 hyperintense lesion in the dorsal cervical cord at C3-C4, spanning approximately 12 mm. Morphology and location are consistent with a demyelinating plaque. This finding, in combination with the intracranial lesions, supports dissemination in space (DIS) per the McDonald 2017 criteria and raises the likelihood of a demyelinating etiology over a neoplastic one for the intracranial mass.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Most likely given morphology and location
Less likely given absence of leptomeningeal enhancement

recommended_actions: Correlate with brain MRI findings for assessment of DIS criteria
Consider repeat imaging in 3 months to assess for DIT

Demyelinating Antibodies.clinical_significance: Negative AQP4 antibodies effectively exclude NMOSD in most cases
Negative MOG antibodies argue against MOGAD, which can present with tumefactive lesions

output.interpretation: Both aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies are negative. This excludes NMOSD and MOGAD as alternative diagnoses. In the context of a tumefactive lesion with demyelinating features, the negative antibody panel supports a diagnosis of tumefactive multiple sclerosis rather than an antibody-mediated demyelinating disorder.

findings.type: Perfusion analysis of left parietal lesion

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Mildly elevated at the enhancing rim (rCBV ratio 1.4 relative to contralateral normal white matter) but significantly lower than typically seen in high-grade gliomas (usually >1.75)
signal_characteristics.Ktrans: Mildly elevated permeability at the enhancing margin
signal_characteristics.CBF: Mildly increased at the rim, low centrally

additional_observations: The rCBV ratio of 1.4 is below the threshold typically associated with high-grade glioma (>1.75) and is more consistent with tumefactive demyelination or low-grade glioma
No arteriovenous shunting or neovascularity identified
output.impression: Perfusion MRI demonstrates mildly elevated rCBV at the enhancing rim (ratio 1.4) but below the threshold typical for high-grade glioma. This pattern is more consistent with tumefactive demyelination than with glioblastoma, though low-grade glioma cannot be fully excluded by perfusion characteristics alone.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: More likely given rCBV below glioma threshold

recommended_actions: Tissue diagnosis remains recommended if clinical course is not consistent with demyelination
Consider short-interval follow-up MRI after empiric corticosteroid trial

Histopathology.value: Active demyelinating lesion with sharply demarcated borders
Histopathology.clinical_significance: Consistent with tumefactive demyelination, not neoplasm

Histopathology.value: Abundant foamy macrophages (CD68+) containing myelin debris
Histopathology.clinical_significance: Active myelin phagocytosis indicative of acute demyelination
Reactive process, not neoplastic astrocytic proliferation

Histopathology.value: Marked myelin loss in lesion center with relative preservation at edges
Histopathology.clinical_significance: Demyelination pattern rather than destruction from tumor infiltration

Histopathology.value: Relative axonal preservation within areas of myelin loss
Histopathology.clinical_significance: Key distinguishing feature from glioma: axons relatively spared despite severe myelin loss
Low proliferative index argues strongly against high-grade neoplasm
No molecular evidence of diffuse glioma

Histopathology.value: Prominent perivascular lymphocytic cuffing (CD3+ T cells predominant)

output.interpretation: Histopathologic examination reveals features diagnostic of active demyelinating disease: abundant foamy macrophages with myelin debris, reactive gliosis, marked myelin loss with relative axonal preservation, and prominent perivascular lymphocytic infiltration. There is no evidence of neoplasm (low Ki-67, no IDH mutation, no atypical glial cells). These findings are consistent with tumefactive multiple sclerosis (Creutzfeldt-Peters pattern). The diagnosis of CNS glioma is effectively excluded.
abnormal_values_summary: Relative axonal preservation despite myelin loss
Low Ki-67 and no IDH mutation exclude glioma

Visual Evoked Potentials.clinical_significance: Prolonged P100 latency suggests subclinical optic nerve demyelination in the left eye, despite normal visual acuity

output.interpretation: Visual evoked potentials reveal a prolonged P100 latency and reduced amplitude in the left eye, indicative of subclinical optic nerve demyelination. The right eye is normal. This finding provides additional evidence for dissemination in space, supporting a diagnosis of MS even in the absence of clinical optic neuritis.
abnormal_values_summary: Prolonged P100 latency in left eye (118 ms) suggesting subclinical optic nerve demyelination
Reduced P100 amplitude in left eye (4.2 microV)

output.query: tumefactive multiple sclerosis versus glioma differential diagnosis MRI features

results.title: Tumefactive Demyelinating Lesions: Conventional and Advanced MRI Features
results.authors: Lucchinetti CF, Gavrilova RH, Metz I, et al.
results.summary: Tumefactive demyelinating lesions (>2 cm) can mimic brain neoplasms. Key MRI features favoring demyelination include: open-ring (incomplete) enhancement, leading-edge restricted diffusion, and relatively low rCBV on perfusion imaging compared to gliomas.

results.title: Distinguishing Tumefactive MS from CNS Neoplasm: Role of MR Spectroscopy and Perfusion
results.authors: Kiriyama T, Kataoka H, Kasai T, et al.
results.summary: MRS in tumefactive MS may show elevated choline and decreased NAA similar to gliomas, making spectroscopy alone insufficient for diagnosis. However, elevated myo-inositol and glutamate/glutamine ratios favor demyelination. Perfusion showing rCBV <1.75 supports demyelination.

results.title: Tumefactive Multiple Sclerosis: An Uncommon Diagnostic Challenge
results.summary: Tumefactive MS is defined as demyelinating lesions >2 cm with mass effect, edema, and/or ring enhancement. May occur as the first demyelinating event. Biopsy should be interpreted cautiously as reactive changes can mimic low-grade glioma. Absence of OCBs occurs in 5-10% of MS patients.

output.summary: Tumefactive multiple sclerosis presents with large (>2 cm) demyelinating lesions that can closely mimic brain neoplasms. Key distinguishing MRI features include incomplete (open) ring enhancement, leading-edge restricted diffusion, and low rCBV on perfusion imaging. MR spectroscopy alone is insufficient to differentiate from glioma. Biopsy with careful histopathological interpretation remains the gold standard when imaging is equivocal. Approximately 5-10% of confirmed MS patients lack oligoclonal bands in CSF.

output.proposed: Methylprednisolone 1g IV daily for 5 days (pulse corticosteroid therapy for tumefactive MS)
interactions: Oral contraceptives: corticosteroids may reduce efficacy of hormonal contraception; counsel patient regarding backup contraception
Ibuprofen: increased risk of GI ulceration and bleeding with concurrent corticosteroid and NSAID use
warnings: Monitor blood glucose closely during high-dose corticosteroid therapy
Consider GI prophylaxis with proton pump inhibitor during pulse steroid course
Psychiatric side effects (insomnia, agitation, psychosis) can occur with high-dose IV methylprednisolone
Taper should be individualized; abrupt discontinuation after prolonged use may cause adrenal crisis
alternatives: Plasma exchange (PLEX) if corticosteroid response is inadequate
IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) as second-line if PLEX not available

Optical Coherence Tomography.clinical_significance: RNFL thinning in the left eye suggests prior subclinical optic nerve injury, consistent with demyelinating disease
Ganglion cell loss corroborates subclinical optic neuropathy

output.interpretation: OCT reveals thinning of the retinal nerve fiber layer and ganglion cell layer in the left eye, indicative of prior subclinical optic nerve damage. This finding is consistent with prior subclinical optic neuritis and supports dissemination in space for a diagnosis of MS. The right eye is within normal limits.
abnormal_values_summary: Left eye RNFL thinning (72 micrometers) suggesting prior optic nerve injury
Left eye ganglion cell layer reduction (68 micrometers)

differential.key_distinguishing: Ring enhancement in gliomas typically complete rather than open-ring; rCBV usually >1.75; progressive course without remission; biopsy shows neoplastic astrocytes with high Ki-67
differential.likelihood: Initially most likely given size, mass effect, and family history of GBM, but excluded by histopathology and perfusion MRI

differential.key_distinguishing: Typically shows homogeneous enhancement rather than ring enhancement; restricted diffusion is more uniform; CSF cytology or flow cytometry positive for monoclonal B cells
differential.likelihood: Less likely given open-ring enhancement pattern and negative CSF cytology/flow cytometry

differential.key_distinguishing: AQP4 antibodies positive; longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>3 vertebral segments); optic neuritis predominant presentation
differential.likelihood: Excluded by negative AQP4 antibodies and short spinal cord lesion

differential.key_distinguishing: MOG antibodies positive; tumefactive lesions can occur but typically better recovery; optic neuritis with disc edema common
Ring enhancement with restricted diffusion centrally (not at leading edge); fever, leukocytosis, elevated CRP/ESR; history of systemic infection or immunosuppression
differential.likelihood: Unlikely given normal inflammatory markers, no fever, and pattern of restricted diffusion at periphery rather than center

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with contrast and MR spectroscopy to characterize the intracranial lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large tumefactive lesion with open-ring enhancement, leading-edge restricted diffusion, and MRS showing elevated choline/decreased NAA; small periventricular lesion also noted

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, coagulation panel, tumor markers, ANA, and vitamin B12
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs within normal limits, no evidence of infection, inflammation, or systemic malignancy

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including cytology, flow cytometry, oligoclonal bands, IgG index, and myelin basic protein
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild pleocytosis, elevated protein, absent OCBs, normal IgG index, elevated MBP, negative cytology - non-specific but elevated MBP suggests demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to assess for dissemination in space
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Single non-enhancing demyelinating lesion at C3-C4, supporting DIS criteria

optimal_actions.action: Request aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both AQP4 and MOG antibodies negative, excluding antibody-mediated demyelinating disorders

optimal_actions.action: Order perfusion MRI to help distinguish tumefactive demyelination from high-grade glioma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: rCBV ratio 1.4 (below glioma threshold of 1.75), favoring demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Consider stereotactic biopsy for tissue diagnosis given diagnostic uncertainty
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Histopathology shows active demyelination with foamy macrophages, axonal preservation, low Ki-67, no IDH mutation - diagnostic of tumefactive MS

optimal_actions.action: Initiate IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy after demyelinating etiology is confirmed
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Check interactions with current medications; counsel regarding GI prophylaxis and glucose monitoring

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on tumefactive MS versus glioma to guide diagnostic approach
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Literature confirms open-ring enhancement, low rCBV, and leading-edge diffusion restriction as features of tumefactive MS; OCB-negative MS occurs in 5-10%

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials and OCT to assess for subclinical optic nerve involvement
optimal_actions.expected_finding: VEP shows prolonged P100 in left eye; OCT shows RNFL thinning - subclinical optic neuritis supports DIS

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the mass lesion
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including cytology to evaluate for neoplasm
Order AQP4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude antibody-mediated demyelinating disorders
Obtain tissue diagnosis (biopsy) given diagnostic uncertainty between neoplasm and demyelination
Initiate corticosteroid therapy once demyelinating etiology is established
Order spine MRI to assess for dissemination in space
Neurosurgical consultation for mass effect and potential decompression
contraindicated_actions: Initiating empiric chemotherapy or radiation without tissue diagnosis
Performing extensive surgical resection assuming glioma without frozen section confirmation
Starting disease-modifying therapy (e.g., natalizumab, fingolimod) before confirming MS diagnosis
Administering thrombolytics (tPA) for presumed ischemic stroke
Dismissing the case as migraine or functional disorder without completing workup
Performing lumbar puncture without prior imaging in a patient with papilledema and mass effect (risk of herniation)
key_reasoning_points: The large size (>2 cm), mass effect, and ring enhancement of the lesion initially mimic a brain neoplasm, especially glioblastoma, making this a diagnostic puzzle

The incomplete (open-ring) enhancement pattern with the gap facing cortical gray matter is a key MRI feature that distinguishes tumefactive MS from glioma, where ring enhancement is typically complete
Leading-edge restricted diffusion (at the advancing border of demyelination) is characteristic of tumefactive MS, unlike gliomas where restricted diffusion reflects high cellularity
MR spectroscopy showing elevated choline and decreased NAA is a significant red herring because it overlaps with neoplasm; elevated myo-inositol provides a subtle clue toward demyelination
The family history of glioblastoma in the father is a major red herring that biases toward a neoplastic diagnosis
Absence of oligoclonal bands and normal IgG index in CSF creates additional diagnostic uncertainty, as these are typically positive in MS but are absent in 5-10% of confirmed cases
Elevated myelin basic protein in CSF provides a subtle but important clue pointing toward active demyelination
The small non-enhancing periventricular lesion on the contralateral side is an easily overlooked finding that hints at a multifocal demyelinating process rather than a solitary neoplasm
Perfusion MRI showing rCBV below the glioma threshold (1.4 vs >1.75) is an important differentiating feature
Definitive diagnosis requires histopathologic confirmation showing demyelination with axonal preservation, distinguishing from glioma where axons are destroyed by infiltrating tumor cells
The absence of papilledema with mass effect necessitates careful CSF acquisition planning due to herniation risk

metadata.difficulty_description: Initial presentation mimics high-grade glioma with large ring-enhancing lesion, mass effect, and a family history of GBM as a strong red herring. CSF lacks typical MS markers (no oligoclonal bands, normal IgG index). MRS findings overlap with neoplasm. The correct diagnosis requires recognizing subtle imaging features (open-ring enhancement, leading-edge diffusion restriction), pursuing tissue diagnosis, and integrating findings from multiple modalities including a small contralateral periventricular lesion that hints at multifocal demyelination.
